subtopic_detail: Incomplete Kawasaki disease recognition and IVIG timing
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-year-old boy with 6 days of high fevers above 39 °C unresponsive to paracetamol. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, dry cracked lips and a single tender enlarged cervical lymph node. A polymorphous truncal rash appeared yesterday. He has no peripheral oedema and no peeling. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and he is fully immunised. Throat swab and blood cultures are pending.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 °C, HR 156, RR 32, SpO2 98% room air, BP 96/58
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, cracked red lips, anterior cervical node 1.5 cm tender, polymorphous trunk rash; no peripheral oedema or peeling
data_table.Investigations: CRP 110 mg/L, ESR 80 mm/h, platelets 540 x10^9/L, ALT 78, albumin 28 g/L, urine WCC 30 x10^6/L sterile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and review in 48 hours if the fever persists today
options.rationale: Delayed treatment beyond day 10 of illness markedly increases coronary aneurysm rate; IVIG must be given promptly when the criteria are met. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Treat with intravenous flucloxacillin for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.rationale: Scalded skin syndrome presents with positive Nikolsky sign and fragile skin, not non-purulent conjunctivitis, cervical adenopathy and oral changes; the picture fits Kawasaki disease. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial cervical lymphadenitis and observe
options.rationale: Isolated bacterial adenitis does not produce the multisystem pattern of conjunctivitis, mucositis, rash and sterile pyuria; antibiotics alone will not prevent coronary aneurysm. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus high-dose aspirin and arrange echocardiogram
options.rationale: Incomplete Kawasaki is diagnosed when fever above 5 days plus supportive features and elevated inflammatory markers are present; IVIG within 10 days reduces coronary aneurysm risk. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Start high-dose prednisolone first-line and defer IVIG until clinical response is reviewed
options.rationale: First-line therapy in Australia is IVIG plus aspirin; corticosteroids are added for high-risk or IVIG-resistant cases, not as monotherapy. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when at least 5 days of fever and 2 to 3 classic features are accompanied by supportive laboratory findings such as raised CRP and ESR, thrombocytosis, anaemia, transaminitis, hypoalbuminaemia or sterile pyuria. Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg with aspirin reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk from about 25 percent to under 5 percent if given before day 10 of illness. An echocardiogram should be performed at diagnosis and again at 6 to 8 weeks. Steroids are reserved for high-risk or treatment-resistant disease.
key_points: Treat suspected incomplete Kawasaki with IVIG plus aspirin promptly.
Aim to treat before day 10 of fever to minimise coronary risk.
Repeat echocardiogram at 6 to 8 weeks regardless of initial findings.
Sterile pyuria is a useful supportive feature in incomplete cases.
explanation.pearls: Persistent fever 36 hours after the first IVIG dose defines IVIG resistance and warrants a second IVIG infusion plus consideration of methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg or infliximab in conjunction with paediatric cardiology.

subtopic_detail: Duct-dependent cyanotic neonatal cardiac lesion presentation
stem: You are the neonatal registrar called to the postnatal ward at hour 36 of life. A term male is increasingly cyanosed and lethargic. He passed initial pulse oximetry screening. He now has central cyanosis, weak pulses in all four limbs and a hyperdynamic precordium without murmur. Pre-ductal SpO2 is 65% and post-ductal SpO2 is 64% in air, unchanged on 100% oxygen for 10 minutes. Capillary blood gas shows pH 7.18, lactate 7.2 mmol/L, base excess -12, normal glucose.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 °C, HR 178, RR 70, SpO2 65% pre-ductal, 64% post-ductal, BP 56/34 mean 41
data_table.Examination: Central cyanosis, weak pulses all four limbs, no murmur, hepatomegaly 2 cm; no respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: ECG normal QRS axis; CXR mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette, oligaemic lung fields

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion at 10 nanograms/kg/min and call cardiology
options.rationale: Failed hyperoxia with cyanosis in the first 48 hours indicates a duct-dependent cyanotic lesion; prostaglandin E1 maintains ductal patency until definitive diagnosis and surgery. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 100% by nasal high flow and admit to special care nursery for monitoring
options.rationale: Persistent cyanosis on 100% FiO2 over 10 minutes is the failed hyperoxia test diagnostic of cyanotic congenital heart disease; oxygen alone will not address the lesion. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Treat for presumed early-onset sepsis with intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin only
options.rationale: Sepsis can mimic this presentation but failed hyperoxia and metabolic acidosis with hepatomegaly suggest a duct-dependent lesion that needs immediate prostaglandin therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 30 minutes to correct the lactic acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not treat the underlying ductal-dependent circulation; restoring ductal patency with prostaglandin corrects perfusion and resolves acidosis. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

options.text: Commence dobutamine 5 micrograms/kg/min as inotropic support before any other measure
options.rationale: Inotropes do not restore ductal-dependent pulmonary blood flow and may worsen the shunt; prostaglandin E1 is the priority intervention with cardiology input. (Source: RCH CPG Cyanotic neonate)

explanation.summary: A neonate with cyanosis and failed hyperoxia in the first 48 hours of life has duct-dependent congenital heart disease until proven otherwise. Prostaglandin E1 reopens or maintains the ductus arteriosus and restores pulmonary or systemic blood flow until echocardiography defines anatomy and surgical or catheter intervention is arranged. Side effects to anticipate during prostaglandin therapy include apnoea, hypotension and fever. Septic workup and broad spectrum antibiotics may be added because sepsis can mimic duct-dependent collapse.
key_points: Failed hyperoxia means SpO2 stays under 85% on 100% oxygen.
Start prostaglandin E1 at 10 nanograms/kg/min and titrate.
Anticipate apnoea, hypotension and fever as side effects.
Echocardiography defines the lesion and guides intervention.
explanation.pearls: Prostaglandin E1 causes apnoea in about 10 to 15 percent of neonates; have a clinician at the bedside able to intubate before starting infusion, particularly during interhospital retrieval.

subtopic_detail: Group B streptococcus intrapartum prophylaxis with severe penicillin allergy
stem: You are the obstetric registrar attending the birth suite for a 30-year-old primigravida in labour at 39 weeks. Her vaginal-rectal GBS screen at 36 weeks was positive. Membranes ruptured 2 hours ago and she is now 5 cm dilated. Her booking notes record an episode of anaphylaxis after intramuscular benzylpenicillin in childhood with lip swelling, wheeze and a brief loss of consciousness. She has had no antibiotics since then. There is no other significant past history and the foetus is reassuring on CTG.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air, BP 122/74
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, vertex at -1 station, intact CTG reassuring, no chorioamnionitis features
data_table.Investigations: Penicillin sensitivity testing not previously performed; GBS rectovaginal swab positive at 36 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis?

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g loading then 1 g every 8 hours until birth as standard option
options.rationale: Cefazolin is recommended for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy only; documented anaphylaxis makes cephalosporin cross-reactivity unacceptable without confirmed testing. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 600 mg every 8 hours after confirmed clindamycin susceptibility result
options.rationale: Clindamycin is appropriate only after the GBS isolate has been confirmed susceptible because of high erythromycin and clindamycin resistance rates among Australian isolates. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Intramuscular benzathine penicillin 1.2 million units single dose as soon as labour starts
options.rationale: Long-acting benzathine penicillin is not used for intrapartum prophylaxis because adequate amniotic levels are needed within 4 hours of birth and the route is contraindicated in penicillin anaphylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Withhold intrapartum antibiotics and review the neonate clinically every 4 hours until 48 hours
options.rationale: Known GBS colonisation in labour mandates effective intrapartum prophylaxis to reduce early-onset neonatal sepsis; observation alone is unsafe in this setting. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 20 mg/kg every 8 hours until birth at the recommended infusion rate
options.rationale: Intravenous vancomycin is the recommended option when the woman has confirmed penicillin anaphylaxis and clindamycin susceptibility is unknown, providing reliable GBS cover. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

explanation.summary: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is indicated for women colonised with group B streptococcus to reduce early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis. Benzylpenicillin is first-line; cefazolin is used for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy. With confirmed anaphylaxis to penicillin, vancomycin is recommended unless the isolate has documented clindamycin susceptibility from an antibiogram, in which case clindamycin can be used. Effectiveness requires the antibiotic to be in the maternal circulation for at least 4 hours before birth.
key_points: Use vancomycin for women with anaphylaxis to penicillin.
Use clindamycin only with confirmed susceptibility.
Begin antibiotics with the aim of giving 4 hours before birth.
Cefazolin is only safe in non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy.
explanation.pearls: Australian GBS isolate clindamycin resistance approaches 20 percent, so empirical clindamycin should be avoided in true penicillin anaphylaxis; ask the lab to issue an antibiogram on the colonisation swab early in the third trimester.

subtopic_detail: Indications for antenatal CTG and umbilical artery Doppler in IUGR
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 32-year-old primigravida at 33+0 weeks referred from her midwife with symphysis-fundal height 27 cm against a customised growth chart 10th centile of 31 cm. She has normal blood pressure and no proteinuria. She reports normal foetal movements but feels the baby is smaller. No risk factors for placental insufficiency are identified in her booking. Australian guidelines recommend a structured response when growth restriction is suspected.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 78, RR 16, T 36.6 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 27 cm, head presentation, cephalic, FH 142 reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Pending growth ultrasound and Doppler studies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Continuous cardiotocography for 24 hours as a screening tool for placental insufficiency
options.rationale: Routine prolonged CTG is not a screening test for placental insufficiency; an ultrasound assessment is the appropriate first investigation when growth restriction is suspected. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

options.text: Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein and unconjugated oestriol to assess placental function
options.rationale: Serum analytes are not used to guide management of suspected late third-trimester growth restriction; targeted ultrasound is the recommended pathway. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the foetus to characterise placental and brain morphology
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for selected anomaly evaluation, not for first-line assessment of suspected growth restriction; ultrasound with Doppler is the standard approach. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

options.text: Plain serial fundal height measurements weekly with no further imaging for two weeks
options.rationale: Serial fundal height alone is inadequate when growth restriction is already suspected on the customised chart; ultrasound is needed to confirm the suspicion. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

options.text: Ultrasound foetal biometry with amniotic fluid index and umbilical artery Doppler study
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends prompt ultrasound including biometry, amniotic fluid index and umbilical artery Doppler when fundal height suggests growth restriction, to confirm and guide timing of birth. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

explanation.summary: Suspected foetal growth restriction in the third trimester should be confirmed by formal ultrasound including biometry, estimated foetal weight against a customised chart, amniotic fluid index and umbilical artery Doppler. A normal umbilical artery Doppler with normal liquor is reassuring and allows close outpatient surveillance, whereas absent or reversed end-diastolic flow requires admission and consideration of early delivery. CTG is a complementary tool but is not the screening modality of choice.
key_points: Ultrasound with biometry and Doppler is the first step.
Umbilical artery Doppler guides timing of delivery.
Use customised growth charts to interpret biometry.
Absent or reversed end-diastolic flow needs admission.
explanation.pearls: Once umbilical artery end-diastolic flow becomes absent, latency to delivery is typically 7 days or less; arrange antenatal corticosteroids and a senior-led plan for timing and mode of birth as soon as it is detected.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum LARC initiation timing after vaginal birth
stem: You are the postnatal ward doctor reviewing a 31-year-old woman day 1 after an uncomplicated vaginal birth of her third baby. She is fully breastfeeding and would like long-acting reversible contraception before discharge. She has no history of VTE, no liver disease and a normal cervical screen 12 months ago. Her previous contraceptive history includes a copper IUD she liked. She lives 4 hours from the nearest clinic and would prefer immediate insertion if safe.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 78, RR 16, T 36.7 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Uterus involuted to umbilicus, lochia rubra moderate, no signs of infection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate contraceptive plan?

options.text: Wait until 6 weeks postpartum and offer combined oral contraception in the meantime
options.rationale: Combined oral contraception is UKMEC 4 in the first 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women due to VTE risk; immediate progestogen LARC is preferable here. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method alone until ovulation returns and then review again later
options.rationale: LAM requires exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoea and infant under 6 months and is less effective than LARC; this woman has requested LARC. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)

options.text: Insert an etonogestrel subdermal implant before discharge after counselled consent today
options.rationale: Progestogen-only methods including the etonogestrel implant are UKMEC 1 postpartum and can be inserted on the postnatal ward before discharge, ideal for this woman. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)

options.text: Insert a copper IUD today on the postnatal ward as her chosen long-acting reversible method
options.rationale: Postpartum interval IUD insertion is recommended between 4 weeks postpartum and earlier insertion outside the immediate post-placental window has higher expulsion rates. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)

options.text: Prescribe a combined contraceptive patch starting today and review again in two weeks at GP
options.rationale: Combined patches contain ethinyloestradiol and are contraindicated in the first 6 weeks postpartum while breastfeeding due to VTE risk. (Source: FSRH UKMEC)

explanation.summary: Progestogen-only methods including the implant and the levonorgestrel intrauterine system can be initiated immediately postpartum and are UKMEC 1 regardless of breastfeeding status. The etonogestrel implant is ideal for women requesting long-acting reversible contraception before discharge, especially when access to follow-up is limited. Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC 4 in the first 6 weeks in breastfeeding women and UKMEC 3 between weeks 6 and 6 months due to VTE and lactation concerns. Interval intrauterine device insertion is generally deferred to 4 weeks postpartum.
key_points: Etonogestrel implant is safe immediately postpartum.
Interval IUD insertion is preferred at 4 weeks or later.
Combined hormonal methods are UKMEC 4 in the first 6 weeks postpartum.
LARC reduces unintended pregnancy in the postnatal period.
explanation.pearls: Australian access programmes such as LARC Express allow immediate postpartum implant insertion in regional hospitals; document return-to-fertility counselling because ovulation can occur within 4 weeks in non-breastfeeding women.
